title: Implement JWKS endpoint, key rotation, and token revocation for JWT infrastructure
description: Harden the JWT authentication infrastructure by implementing a JWKS (JSON Web Key Set) endpoint for key discovery, adding key ID (kid) support, and supporting multiple active keys for seamless key rotation. All services must validate JWTs by fetching the JWKS and matching the kid. Standardize on RS256 for all JWTs across the stack. Implement a token revocation list in Valkey to allow emergency revocation of compromised tokens.
subtasks: Develop a lightweight JWKS endpoint (can be a static file served via a service or ConfigMap-mounted JSON) that exposes all active public keys with kid fields.
Update JWT signing to include a unique kid in the JWT header for each key.
Modify all service authentication middleware to fetch and cache the JWKS, validate the JWT signature using the correct key based on kid, and support multiple keys during rotation.
Standardize JWT signing to RS256 across all services; remove any HMAC-SHA256 usage.
Implement a key rotation process: allow both old and new keys to be valid during a rotation window.
Add a Valkey-backed token revocation list: on revocation, add the JWT jti to a blacklist with expiry matching the token's exp.
Update test strategies to include validation of JWKS endpoint, key rotation, and revocation logic.
Run OWASP ZAP scan against all authentication endpoints to verify no signature validation bypasses.
acceptance_criteria: JWKS endpoint is accessible and exposes all active public keys with correct kid fields.
All services validate JWTs using the JWKS and support multiple keys during rotation.
All JWTs are signed with RS256 and include a kid header.
Key rotation can be performed without downtime; both old and new tokens are accepted during the rotation window.
Revoked tokens are rejected by all services within 60 seconds of revocation.
OWASP ZAP scan shows no signature validation or key confusion vulnerabilities.

title: Integrate identity provider for user authentication and unify user/service JWT handling
description: Establish a user authentication system by integrating an identity provider (e.g., Clerk, Auth0, Keycloak, or Cloudflare Access with JWT minting) for human users. Define the user login flow, token issuance, role assignment, and ensure all services and the frontend can distinguish and handle both user JWTs and service JWTs. Update the Morgan web chat endpoint to enforce authentication tiers, restricting sensitive actions to authenticated/admin users.
subtasks: Select and integrate an identity provider that supports JWT issuance with custom claims for roles.
Implement user registration, login, and session management flows in the frontend, including a login/auth page or redirect.
Update backend auth middleware to accept and validate both user JWTs (from IdP) and service JWTs (from internal signing).
Map RBAC roles from the IdP to the application's role matrix.

Update Morgan's web chat endpoint to allow only general inquiries for unauthenticated users, require verifiable contact info for actions that create records, and restrict financial/reporting tools to authenticated admin sessions.
Implement session abuse detection: rate limit message frequency, detect PII extraction attempts, and flag suspicious conversation patterns.
Add integration tests for user login, role-based access, and Morgan chat endpoint authentication boundaries.
Run security tests to ensure endpoints are not accessible without proper authentication.
acceptance_criteria: Users can register, log in, and receive JWTs with correct roles.
Frontend displays login/auth flow and restricts access to protected pages.
All backend services accept and validate both user and service JWTs, enforcing RBAC consistently.
Morgan web chat endpoint enforces authentication tiers as specified.
Session abuse detection is active and logs suspicious activity.
Integration and security tests confirm that only authorized users can access sensitive actions.

title: Implement secure database credential injection and enforce separation of secrets
description: Enforce secure handling of database credentials by ensuring that passwords are never stored in ConfigMaps or environment variables visible in pod specs. Store only hostnames and ports in ConfigMaps, credentials in Kubernetes Secrets, and use init containers or sidecars to construct full connection URLs at runtime. Add explicit validation in the test strategy to ensure no secret data appears in ConfigMaps.
subtasks: Refactor ConfigMaps to store only non-sensitive connection information (hostnames, ports, database names).
Store all database credentials in separate Kubernetes Secrets per service user.

Implement an init container or sidecar (e.g., vault-agent) that reads the Secret and ConfigMap, constructs the full DATABASE_URL, and writes it to a shared emptyDir volume for the main container to consume.
Update service deployments to mount the constructed DATABASE_URL from the shared volume, not from environment variables.
Add CI/CD validation to scan ConfigMaps for accidental inclusion of secrets.
Update documentation and test strategy to require validation that no secret data appears in ConfigMaps.
acceptance_criteria: No database credentials are present in any ConfigMap or environment variable visible in pod specs.
All services receive their full DATABASE_URL via a secure runtime mechanism.
CI/CD pipeline fails if any secret data is detected in ConfigMaps.
Manual and automated tests confirm that secrets are only present in Kubernetes Secrets.

title: Harden Stripe webhook endpoint with strict signature validation and rate limiting
description: Ensure the Stripe webhook endpoint is secure by always validating the Stripe-Signature header before processing any event, returning 400 on invalid signatures, using a custom Axum extractor for raw body access, applying separate rate limiting, and logging all webhook events (including rejections) to the audit log.
subtasks: Implement a custom Axum extractor that captures the raw request body before deserialization.
Use stripe-rust's Webhook::construct_event to validate the Stripe-Signature header for every request.
Return HTTP 400 immediately on invalid signatures; do not process the event.
Apply a dedicated rate limiter to the webhook endpoint (e.g., 50 requests per minute).
Log all webhook events, including rejected ones, to the audit log with request metadata.
Add an integration test that sends a webhook with an invalid signature and verifies it is rejected with 400.
Run an external scan (e.g., OWASP ZAP) to verify the endpoint is not vulnerable to replay or signature bypass attacks.
acceptance_criteria: Webhook handler always validates Stripe-Signature and rejects invalid requests with 400.
Raw body is available for signature verification before any deserialization.
Rate limiting is enforced on the webhook endpoint.
All webhook events are logged to the audit log, including rejections.
Integration test for invalid signature passes.
OWASP ZAP scan shows no signature validation or replay vulnerabilities.

title: Mitigate SQL injection risk in dynamic query builders across all services
description: Ensure all dynamic SQL query builders use parameterized queries, validate filter and sort parameters against allowlists, and add static analysis rules to detect unsafe patterns. Include SQL injection test cases in each service's test strategy.
subtasks: Review all dynamic query builder code (Rust sqlx, Go pgx) to ensure parameterization is enforced and no user input is concatenated into SQL strings.
Validate all enum-type filter values (e.g., status, category) against a whitelist before including in queries.
Validate sort column names against an explicit allowlist to prevent ORDER BY injection.
Add Semgrep custom rules for Rust sqlx and Go pgx to detect raw SQL string concatenation.
Update test strategies to include SQL injection test cases for all endpoints that accept user input.
Run static analysis and dynamic security tests (e.g., sqlmap, ZAP) against all affected endpoints.
acceptance_criteria: All dynamic queries are parameterized; no user input is directly interpolated into SQL.
Filter and sort parameters are validated against allowlists.
Semgrep rules are in place and CI fails on detection of unsafe patterns.
SQL injection test cases pass (no injection possible).
Static and dynamic security scans show no SQL injection vulnerabilities.

title: Implement log scrubbing and PII classification policy for all service logs
description: Define a PII classification policy and implement log scrubbing across all services. Create a shared log sanitizer to redact sensitive fields, define an allowlist for audit_log.request_metadata, and configure log retention policies in line with GDPR.
subtasks: Draft and document a PII classification policy specifying which fields are considered sensitive and must be redacted.
Implement a shared log sanitizer library for all stacks (Go, Rust, Node.js) that redacts email addresses, phone numbers, credit card patterns, and names from log output.
Update all services to use the log sanitizer before writing to logs or audit logs.
Define and enforce an allowlist for fields in audit_log.request_metadata; blocklist is not sufficient.
For the MCP tool server, explicitly list and redact sensitive input fields (e.g., customer_email, credit card info) in logs.
Configure log retention policies: 90 days for operational logs, 7 years for audit logs.
Add tests to verify that logs do not contain PII after scrubbing.
Run a sample data flow and manually inspect logs to confirm redaction.
acceptance_criteria: PII classification policy is documented and accessible.
All logs are scrubbed of sensitive data before storage.
Audit log request_metadata only contains allowlisted fields.
Log retention policies are configured and enforced.
Tests and manual inspection confirm no PII is present in logs.

title: Pin all third-party images and dependencies, enforce supply chain security
description: Pin all third-party container images (e.g., signal-cli-rest-api) to specific SHA digests, set imagePullPolicy to IfNotPresent, and run container scans before deployment. Pin all code dependencies (Cargo.lock, go.sum, bun.lockb, package.json) and enforce lockfile usage in CI. Add Dependabot/Snyk configuration for automated dependency scanning.
subtasks: Update all Kubernetes manifests to use SHA-pinned image references for third-party containers (e.g., signal-cli-rest-api@sha256:...).
Set imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent for all containers.
Run Trivy or Snyk scans on all container images before deployment.
Consider building custom images from source for critical third-party dependencies.
Add all images to the Kyverno/Gatekeeper allowed registries policy.
Commit lock files for all language stacks (Cargo.lock, go.sum, bun.lockb, package.json).
Update CI to use --frozen-lockfile or equivalent to prevent lockfile drift.
Configure Dependabot/Snyk for all repositories and ensure merge blockers for critical vulnerabilities.
Run a full supply chain security scan and document results.
acceptance_criteria: All third-party images are SHA-pinned and pass container security scans.
All dependencies are pinned and lock files are committed.
CI enforces lockfile usage and fails on drift.
Dependabot/Snyk is active and blocks merges on critical findings.
Supply chain scan reports no unpinned or vulnerable dependencies.

title: Harden file upload endpoint with comprehensive security controls
description: Add comprehensive validation to the file upload endpoint: enforce file size and count limits, validate content-type by magic bytes, reject or sanitize SVG files, strip EXIF data, and configure R2/CDN to serve images securely.
subtasks: Enforce a maximum file size (e.g., 50MB per file) and a maximum number of files per request (e.g., 20).
Validate content-type by reading magic bytes, not just MIME header.
Reject SVG files or sanitize them to prevent XSS.
Strip EXIF data from images before storing, especially GPS and PII fields.
Set Content-Disposition: attachment on non-image content types in R2.
Configure R2/CDN to serve images with Content-Type: image/* only and add Content-Security-Policy headers to prevent script execution.
Add tests for oversized files, SVG uploads, and EXIF stripping.
Run a security scan (e.g., ZAP) against the upload endpoint.
acceptance_criteria: Uploads exceeding size/count limits are rejected.
Only valid image files (by magic bytes) are accepted.
SVG files are either rejected or sanitized.
All EXIF data is stripped from stored images.
R2/CDN serves images securely with correct headers.
Security scan shows no file upload or XSS vulnerabilities.

title: Centralize and enforce RBAC with shared library and integration tests
description: Create a shared RBAC library/crate for all services to ensure consistent enforcement of role-based access control. Add integration tests per service to verify RBAC for each endpoint. Implement additional controls for the morgan-agent role, including rate limiting, audit logging, and anomaly alerting.
subtasks: Develop a shared RBAC library/crate that all services import for permission checks.
Update all services to use the shared RBAC library for endpoint authorization.
Add integration tests for each service to verify that endpoints enforce RBAC as defined in the ConfigMap.
For the morgan-agent role, implement rate limiting on API calls, log all tool invocations to the audit log, and add alerting for unusual access patterns.
Document the RBAC enforcement and testing strategy.
acceptance_criteria: All services use the shared RBAC library for authorization.
Integration tests confirm correct RBAC enforcement for all endpoints.
Morgan-agent role is rate-limited and all actions are logged and monitored.
RBAC enforcement is consistent and auditable across the stack.

title: Enable Valkey authentication and restrict command access
description: Enable authentication on Valkey by setting a strong password via requirepass, configure ACLs for per-service users with limited command sets, update all service connection strings to include credentials, and disable dangerous commands in production.
subtasks: Set a strong password for Valkey using requirepass, store it as an ExternalSecret.
Configure ACLs for each service user, limiting allowed commands (e.g., GET/SET/DEL only).
Update all service connection strings to include authentication credentials.
Disable dangerous commands (FLUSHALL, FLUSHDB, KEYS, CONFIG) in production.
Test that unauthenticated connections are rejected and only allowed commands are permitted.
Run a security scan to verify no unauthorized access to Valkey.
acceptance_criteria: Valkey requires authentication for all connections.
Each service can only execute permitted commands.
Dangerous commands are disabled in production.
Security scan confirms no unauthorized access.

title: Enforce TLS for all PostgreSQL connections
description: Explicitly configure TLS for all PostgreSQL connections by enabling TLS in CNPG, adding sslmode=require or sslmode=verify-full to all connection strings, distributing the CA certificate to services, and adding a test to confirm non-TLS connections are rejected.
subtasks: Verify that the CNPG cluster has TLS enabled (default in modern CNPG).
Update all database connection strings in ConfigMaps and service configurations to include sslmode=require or sslmode=verify-full.
Distribute the CNPG CA certificate to all services and configure connection pools to verify it.
Add a test in the validation suite to confirm that non-TLS connections are rejected.
Document the TLS enforcement and update deployment instructions.
acceptance_criteria: All database connections use TLS with sslmode=require or sslmode=verify-full.
Services fail to connect if TLS is not used.
Validation suite confirms non-TLS connections are rejected.

title: Implement security event alerting and anomaly detection in monitoring
description: Add security-focused AlertManager rules for authentication failures, privilege escalation attempts, unusual API access patterns, audit log anomalies, database permission denied events, and network policy violations. Create a Grafana security dashboard for visibility.
subtasks: Define AlertManager rules for: >50 HTTP 401s in 5 minutes, >20 HTTP 403s from a single service in 5 minutes, Cilium network policy deny events, SELECT/DELETE on audit schema, failed database authentication attempts.
Configure alert routing to appropriate channels (e.g., Slack, PagerDuty).
Create a Grafana dashboard showing auth failures, RBAC denials, and network policy violations over time.
Test alerting by simulating each event type and verifying alerts are triggered.
Document the security monitoring and response process.
acceptance_criteria: All specified security alerts are active and routed correctly.
Grafana dashboard displays real-time security metrics.
Simulated events trigger alerts as expected.

title: Secure GDPR data export URLs and add access controls
description: Add access controls to GDPR data exports by serving exports through an authenticated API endpoint, using shorter signed URL expiry, encrypting export files at rest, adding revocation mechanisms, and logging all accesses.
subtasks: Replace direct R2 signed URLs with an authenticated API endpoint that verifies requester authorization and logs downloads.
Reduce signed URL expiry to 24-48 hours.
Encrypt export files at rest with a customer-specific key.
Implement a mechanism to revoke/invalidate export URLs before expiry.
Log every access to the export URL in the audit log.
Send a notification when an export is downloaded.
Test that only authorized users can access exports and that revocation works.
acceptance_criteria: Exports are only accessible via authenticated API endpoint.
Signed URLs expire within 48 hours and can be revoked.
All accesses are logged and notifications are sent.
Unauthorized access attempts are rejected.

title: Enforce secure WebSocket chat authentication and session controls
description: Define and enforce explicit authentication tiers for the Morgan web chat WebSocket endpoint. Restrict sensitive actions to authenticated/admin users, require verifiable contact info for record-creating actions, and implement session abuse detection.
subtasks: Update the WebSocket chat endpoint to allow only general inquiries and equipment browsing for unauthenticated users.
Require users to provide verifiable contact info for actions that create records (e.g., generate_quote, create_invoice, vet_customer).
Restrict MCP tools that access financial data to authenticated admin sessions only.
Implement session abuse detection: rate limit message frequency, detect PII extraction attempts, and flag suspicious conversation patterns.
Add tests to verify that sensitive actions are blocked for unauthenticated users and that abuse detection is active.
acceptance_criteria: Sensitive actions are only available to authenticated/admin users.
Session abuse detection is active and logs suspicious activity.
Tests confirm that unauthenticated users cannot access restricted actions.

title: Enforce image and dependency pinning for supply chain security
description: Ensure all container images and code dependencies are pinned to specific versions or digests. Enforce this via admission policies and CI checks.
subtasks: Update all Kubernetes manifests to use SHA-pinned image references.
Set imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent for all containers.
Add CI checks to fail builds if unpinned images or dependencies are detected.
Run container and dependency scans as part of CI/CD pipeline.
Document the pinning policy and enforcement mechanisms.
acceptance_criteria: All images and dependencies are pinned.
CI/CD pipeline fails on unpinned references.
Admission policies block unpinned images.
